title: Driving with Autonomous Aids
description: This dataset comprises physiological and behavioral recordings from a semi-automated driving task designed to investigate trust in automation (TiA). Participants completed lane maintenance, following distance control, and collision avoidance maneuvers with optional autonomous driving assistance under varying performance conditions. The study employed multimodal psychophysiological monitoring (EEG, EOG, ECG, EDA) and behavioral metrics to develop quantitative indicators for real-time assessment and prediction of operator trust dynamics in human-automation interaction.
methods_description: Participants completed a semi-automated driving task involving lane maintenance, following distance from a lead vehicle, and collision avoidance with oncoming traffic and pedestrians. Multimodal physiological data were recorded including EEG, electrooculography (EOG), electrocardiography (ECG), electrodermal activity (EDA), and gaze position tracking. Behavioral data included subject vehicle control responses and button presses for collision avoidance. Six experimental conditions were tested: practice baseline, manual driving, full autonomy (good and bad performance), and speed-only autonomy (good and bad performance).
